title: VPN Service Subscription Test with Burner Email and Velocity Escalation
narrative: Fraudster tests a stolen card by subscribing to a privacy-focused VPN service, typically $9.99-$14.99 monthly trials. The attacker uses a disposable email domain (guerrillamail.com, tempmail.org, 10minutemail.com) and immediately attempts 2-4 additional subscriptions at different VPN/privacy providers within 45 minutes. The P_emaildomain shows throwaway pattern while card1 shows first transaction ever recorded in system. After successful VPN authorization, fraudster pivots to higher-value digital goods within 2-6 hours. Transactions cluster in late night hours (22:00-04:00 local card time) with dist1 showing 800+ km from card billing addr1. Card1_txn_count_24hr jumps from 0 to 5+, all ProductCD='S' (services), with identical device_id but rotating IP addresses suggesting proxy rotation. No prior relationship between card1 and any R_emaildomain in merchant history.
indicators: TransactionAmt=$9.99-$14.99 at MCC 4816 (VPN/privacy service) with card1_txn_count_lifetime=1
card1_txn_count_6hr increases from 1 to 4-6 transactions, all ProductCD='S', all different merchants
txn_hour between 22-04 with is_night=1 and dist1>800km from card addr1
Identical device_id across transactions but id_31 (browser signature) shows 3+ unique values indicating proxy/VPN rotation
Zero historical overlap between card1 and any P_emaildomain or R_emaildomain in merchant database
Time delta between first VPN auth and next high-value transaction (>$50) is 2-6 hours
summary: A card's very first transaction is a low-cost VPN subscription using a disposable email address, followed rapidly by 4-6 similar service purchases at different merchants within hours, all from the same device but rotating IP addresses over 800km from the billing address during late-night hours.
reasoning: The attacker uses cheap VPN trials as a low-risk test to confirm the stolen card works before moving to higher-value fraud, while burner emails, proxy rotation, and geographic mismatches reveal deliberate attempts to hide identity and evade detection rather than legitimate privacy-conscious behavior.
indicator_explanations: First-ever transaction is a cheap VPN subscription to hide future activity
Email is a throwaway service designed to evade merchant contact attempts
Card velocity explodes from single test to multi-merchant attack within hours
Transactions happen overnight from location far from cardholder's registered address
Same device but browser fingerprint keeps changing through VPN or proxy rotation
Card and email addresses have never appeared together in merchant records
Attacker waits a few hours after VPN setup before hitting high-value targets
